title: Is This Not the Path of Affection Shown by Anna?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's organisational argument, quoted explanations, humour, analogy, political directness, and paragraph order. The Tamil reading copy below restores the source-printed opening salutation, rejoins the scan-confirmed sentence about forthcoming district, union and town elections, and corrects clear OCR character and word-boundary errors. The canonical source document remains unchanged. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: After the Kazhagam lost its opportunity for victory in the parliamentary election, I wrote two letters to Udanpirappus: "You Will Understand the Subtle Truth" on 27 June 2014, and "Let Us Preserve Periyar's Discipline and Labour in Anna's Path" on 29 June. This is the third letter in that sequence.

When the Kazhagam lost in every constituency, our opponents, as usual, indulged in wishful thinking. They imagined that it was all over; the DMK could never rise again; all its frontline leaders would leave; there was no need even to speak of the cadres, who would join their parties by the thousands; and the DMK leadership would never stand again. Only after seeing that not even one grassroots worker, one of the Kazhagam's deep taproots, had moved did they begin to reconsider.

Echoing the wishes of our opponents, some weeklies and one or two newspapers deliberately spread fabricated reports that Kazhagam candidates had been defeated by the party's own frontline functionaries, who had failed to work vigorously and had accepted money from opponents to betray the party. They published contrived claims that some district secretaries had become feudal chieftains and must face action, adding that the AIADMK would have thrown them out by now.

They even published the names of eighteen district secretaries as a supposed list of those against whom the DMK was going to act. Another publication carried a lengthy article titled "Remove Every District Secretary." Their intention was not to purify the Kazhagam. They hoped that if the DMK took action, the party might split, those disciplined might unite and leave as a group, and the Kazhagam might break apart. That vain hope was why they published such baseless reports.

Only after this did we convene the DMK High-Level Strategy Committee to discuss action. One member looked at me and said: "Thalaivar, you certainly will not act against anyone. Your heart is too tender; you cannot bring yourself to act against anyone."

Taking action requires no time at all. As an AIADMK minister himself said in the Assembly, a minister in that party may lose his post by the time he walks outside. But the DMK is not like that. The DMK does not possess a dictatorship that shifts people about at will like dolls in a festival display. The guilty may be punished, but the innocent must not be punished. Therefore, when disciplinary action is considered in the DMK, we seek an explanation, examine what has been written, and then decide democratically.

Elsewhere there is neither explanation nor onion, as the saying goes. If Amma thinks it, that is the end of it. Today Sasikala and her associates may be driven out of the house; tomorrow they may be taken back. If she wishes, Sasikala's husband may be arrested and imprisoned repeatedly.

Accordingly, we asked every Kazhagam candidate to report whether any comrades or frontline functionaries had contributed to their defeat. We separately sought reports from the headquarters representatives appointed to each parliamentary constituency. Those against whom the reports contained numerous accusations and complaints were temporarily suspended and asked to respond within one week. Headquarters informed them that if they did not reply, it would be assumed that they had nothing to say, and permanent action would be taken under the party rules.

Even after this action, of the thirty-three people concerned, all but one refused even to consider leaving on the ground that the DMK had failed to win a single parliamentary seat and that they should seek a fruit-bearing tree elsewhere. That one person had originally come from the other side. We elevated him and even made him a minister, but he revealed his nature. Instead of giving an explanation, he replied by attacking the Kazhagam leadership.
I read the explanations submitted by all the others several times. Let me summarise a few.

Seeni Annadurai wrote: "From my father R. Srinivasan and my late brother, the valiant Seeni Panneerselvam, to me, my family and I have regarded the Kazhagam as our very breath and worked for its growth. In the recent parliamentary election, as always, I worked tirelessly for its victory. I have not engaged in anti-Kazhagam or treacherous activity and never will. The Kazhagam is my very breath."

No. Vai. Muthukumarasamy wrote: "I have never betrayed the Kazhagam in any way. Whatever honour I possess comes from the Kazhagam. My happiness lies in calling myself a DMK man, in being an Udanpirappu of the Kazhagam Thalaivar, and a younger brother of Treasurer Thalapathi. I am one of the lowest-level workers awaiting your command. If headquarters believes there is any charge against me, I sincerely ask forgiveness."

Kozhikkadai N. Ganesan wrote: "When the candidate came for the introductory meeting, I arranged a grand welcome. The police have filed a case against me, the union secretary, because of it. I have never thought of betraying the Kazhagam even by a drop. I am deeply committed to protecting it. I request that the disciplinary action be withdrawn."

Ko. Jayapal wrote: "I accepted Thanthai Periyar's principle of self-respect. I work with the Thalaivar as my life and the Kazhagam as my breath. To this day I have never acted against the Kazhagam or its leader. If I am considered to have committed any error, I humbly ask forgiveness and an opportunity to continue Kazhagam work."

K. Mariappan wrote: "Ayya, I have worked for the party since 2003. From the age at which I gained awareness, the DMK built and protected by Kalaignar has been my life. I will never contemplate betrayal of such a movement. As long as I live, I will labour for the Kazhagam in the path of Thalaivar Kalaignar, the Professor and Thalapathi."

Thendral Selvaraj wrote: "Since joining the Kazhagam in 1980, I have worked sincerely for every candidate it announced and lived with the Kazhagam as my very breath. I am deeply distressed by the present action and bow my head in shame. If the accusations against me are proved, I will accept any punishment you impose. Under no circumstances will I betray our Kazhagam. I trust that the movement and its Thalaivar will never abandon a true worker."

S. M. Bose wrote: "In my forty-seven-year political history, I have never given the enemy even the slightest opening against the leadership or movement. The movement is my breath. My heart would never permit me to betray it or its leadership. Even if mistakes occurred knowingly or unknowingly, I ask that I be forgiven and permitted to continue movement work."

Udanpirappē, although I would like to publish summaries of every letter received by headquarters on this issue, they are all written in the same spirit. The leadership has not simply accepted every explanation as entirely true or concluded that every complaint against them was false.

I understand that in a great movement, even though all belong to one organisation, differences may occasionally arise between individuals. Those who dislike us may turn one into ten, a splinter into a pillar, and a small scratch into a great rupture. Ours is a democratic movement. In authoritarian movements, arbitrary announcements may say: today you are a minister, tomorrow get up and leave; today you are district secretary, tomorrow another person is.

Newspapers finish reporting those events in a day. But if even a minor change occurs in our movement, they publish imaginary stories for ten days. Some complaints may contain truth. A few may ask: "Was I alone at fault? Did no one else err? Why is there no action against them? Are we alone to be sacrificed?"

Even when I suspect that one or two complaints may be true, memories come before me despite myself: their past labour for the Kazhagam, the burdens they bore, and the hardships and losses they endured. Some may ask whether action was not taken against people in the past, and whether you did not accept it then. The Kazhagam has taken such action. Only those who understand me know how much pain my heart suffered at those moments.

Some waited in expectation that the DMK would take harsh action, expel many people, drive large numbers to other parties, and eventually cease to exist. I am reminded of a story Perarignar Anna recounted in his long article, "If We Look Only for Faults, We Will Have No Kin."

A physician seeking a livelihood travelled to another town. One morning, he saw many residents working in the fields under the morning sun. He rejoiced. Morning sunlight, he thought, produced an excess of bile; these people would certainly develop bilious illnesses; he would earn a large income. Believing he had found the right place, he returned home and came back that evening with bundles of medicine.

But on arrival his joy vanished and worry took its place. He saw all the townspeople standing in the evening sun. Medical understanding held that evening sunlight removed the excess bile. What could the physician do? Deciding that the town was of no use, he sought another place.

In the same way, those who opened their mouths and drank the milk of fantasy, expecting a split in the Kazhagam, have been disappointed by its action. When temporary suspensions were imposed and explanations sought, they expected no one to reply and everyone to leave. Now they may lament: "Has the action been withdrawn? Is the Kazhagam becoming united again?"
The Kazhagam is not a glass vessel that cannot be repaired once broken. It is a vast reservoir. Water struck by water does not separate.

Those who wanted disciplinary action may be disappointed. But district, union and town organisational elections will soon arrive, offering them reassurance. In those elections, Kazhagam comrades themselves will defeat those who truly committed wrongdoing. If misconduct continues beyond that and complaints keep coming, there will be no alternative to the Kazhagam discipline taught by Periyar. Discipline is the vital centre of the Kazhagam.
If we look only for faults, we will have no kin. If we live together, a million benefits will follow.
With affection,
M. K.
21 July 2014
